instruction: Define 'happy' in context: She was happy to hear the good news.
response: Definition: feeling or showing pleasure or contentment. Etymology: Old English hæp (luck, chance). Usage example: She was happy to hear the good news.

instruction: Define 'run' in context: He runs five miles every morning.
response: Definition: move at a speed faster than a walk. Etymology: Old English rinnan (to flow). Usage example: He runs five miles every morning.

instruction: Define 'big' in context: They live in a big house.
response: Definition: of considerable size or extent. Etymology: Middle English, origin uncertain. Usage example: They live in a big house.

instruction: Define 'make' in context: She will make a cake for the party.
response: Definition: form something by putting parts together. Etymology: Old English macian. Usage example: She will make a cake for the party.

instruction: Define 'go' in context: We go to school by bus.
response: Definition: move from one place to another. Etymology: Old English gan. Usage example: We go to school by bus.

instruction: Define 'good' in context: This is a good opportunity.
response: Definition: to be desired or approved of. Etymology: Old English god. Usage example: This is a good opportunity.

instruction: Define 'new' in context: They bought a new car.
response: Definition: not existing before; made or introduced recently. Etymology: Old English niwe. Usage example: They bought a new car.

instruction: Define 'time' in context: Time flies when you are having fun.
response: Definition: the indefinite continued progress of existence. Etymology: Old English tima. Usage example: Time flies when you are having fun.

instruction: Define 'work' in context: She works at a hospital.
response: Definition: activity involving mental or physical effort. Etymology: Old English weorc. Usage example: She works at a hospital.

instruction: Define 'know' in context: Do you know the answer?
response: Definition: be aware of through observation or inquiry. Etymology: Old English cnawan. Usage example: Do you know the answer?

instruction: Define 'ambiguous' in context: The contract contained several ambiguous clauses.
response: Definition: open to more than one interpretation. Etymology: Latin ambiguus (doubtful). Usage example: The contract contained several ambiguous clauses.

instruction: Define 'benevolent' in context: The benevolent donor funded the new library.
response: Definition: well meaning and kindly. Etymology: Latin bene (well) + volens (wishing). Usage example: The benevolent donor funded the new library.

instruction: Define 'concise' in context: Please write a concise summary of the report.
response: Definition: giving a lot of information clearly in few words. Etymology: Latin concisus (cut up). Usage example: Please write a concise summary of the report.

instruction: Define 'diligent' in context: She is a diligent student who never misses a deadline.
response: Definition: having or showing care in one's work. Etymology: Latin diligens (careful). Usage example: She is a diligent student who never misses a deadline.

instruction: Define 'eloquent' in context: His eloquent speech moved the audience.
response: Definition: fluent or persuasive in speaking or writing. Etymology: Latin eloquens (speaking out). Usage example: His eloquent speech moved the audience.

instruction: Define 'frugal' in context: Living a frugal lifestyle helped him save money.
response: Definition: sparing or economical with money or food. Etymology: Latin frugalis (virtuous). Usage example: Living a frugal lifestyle helped him save money.

instruction: Define 'gregarious' in context: She is gregarious and makes friends easily.
response: Definition: fond of company; sociable. Etymology: Latin gregarius (of a flock). Usage example: She is gregarious and makes friends easily.

instruction: Define 'hinder' in context: Bad weather hindered the rescue operation.
response: Definition: make it difficult for someone to do something. Etymology: Old English hindrian. Usage example: Bad weather hindered the rescue operation.

instruction: Define 'imminent' in context: The storm was imminent, so they stayed indoors.
response: Definition: about to happen. Etymology: Latin imminere (to overhang). Usage example: The storm was imminent, so they stayed indoors.

instruction: Define 'jeopardise' in context: His reckless behaviour jeopardised the project.
response: Definition: put someone or something into a situation of risk. Etymology: Old French jeu parti (divided game). Usage example: His reckless behaviour jeopardised the project.

instruction: Define 'keen' in context: She is keen to learn new skills.
response: Definition: having or showing eagerness or enthusiasm. Etymology: Old English cene (brave). Usage example: She is keen to learn new skills.

instruction: Define 'lucid' in context: The professor gave a lucid explanation of the theory.
response: Definition: expressed clearly; easy to understand. Etymology: Latin lucidus (bright). Usage example: The professor gave a lucid explanation of the theory.

instruction: Define 'meticulous' in context: He is meticulous in his research.
response: Definition: showing great attention to detail. Etymology: Latin meticulosus (fearful). Usage example: He is meticulous in his research.

instruction: Define 'novel' in context: The scientist proposed a novel approach to the problem.
response: Definition: new or unusual in an interesting way. Etymology: Latin novellus (new). Usage example: The scientist proposed a novel approach to the problem.

instruction: Define 'obsolete' in context: Fax machines are becoming obsolete.
response: Definition: no longer produced or used. Etymology: Latin obsoletus (grown old). Usage example: Fax machines are becoming obsolete.

instruction: Define 'abstruse' in context: The philosopher's abstruse arguments confused many readers.
response: Definition: difficult to understand; obscure. Etymology: Latin abstrusus (concealed). Usage example: The philosopher's abstruse arguments confused many readers.

instruction: Define 'acrimony' in context: The divorce proceedings were marked by acrimony.
response: Definition: bitterness or ill feeling. Etymology: Latin acrimonia (sharpness). Usage example: The divorce proceedings were marked by acrimony.

instruction: Define 'bellicose' in context: The bellicose rhetoric alarmed neighbouring countries.
response: Definition: demonstrating aggression and willingness to fight. Etymology: Latin bellicosus (warlike). Usage example: The bellicose rhetoric alarmed neighbouring countries.

instruction: Define 'capricious' in context: The capricious weather ruined their outdoor plans.
response: Definition: given to sudden changes of mood or behaviour. Etymology: Italian capriccio (sudden start). Usage example: The capricious weather ruined their outdoor plans.

instruction: Define 'deleterious' in context: Smoking has deleterious effects on health.
response: Definition: causing harm or damage. Etymology: Greek deleterios (destructive). Usage example: Smoking has deleterious effects on health.

instruction: Define 'ephemeral' in context: Fame can be ephemeral in the age of social media.
response: Definition: lasting for a very short time. Etymology: Greek ephemeros (lasting a day). Usage example: Fame can be ephemeral in the age of social media.

instruction: Define 'fastidious' in context: She is fastidious about the quality of her work.
response: Definition: very attentive to accuracy and detail. Etymology: Latin fastidiosus (disdainful). Usage example: She is fastidious about the quality of her work.

instruction: Define 'garrulous' in context: The garrulous neighbour kept them talking for hours.
response: Definition: excessively talkative. Etymology: Latin garrulus (chattering). Usage example: The garrulous neighbour kept them talking for hours.

instruction: Define 'hegemony' in context: The empire sought hegemony over the entire region.
response: Definition: leadership or dominance of one country over others. Etymology: Greek hegemonia (leadership). Usage example: The empire sought hegemony over the entire region.

instruction: Define 'iconoclast' in context: The iconoclast challenged every established norm.
response: Definition: a person who attacks cherished beliefs. Etymology: Greek eikonoklastes (image breaker). Usage example: The iconoclast challenged every established norm.

instruction: Define 'paradigm' in context: The discovery shifted the scientific paradigm.

instruction: Define 'synthesis' in context: The essay required a synthesis of multiple sources.

instruction: Define 'discourse' in context: Academic discourse requires precise language.

instruction: Define 'gregarious' in context: She is gregarious and makes friends easily.

instruction: Define 'imminent' in context: The storm was imminent, so they stayed indoors.

instruction: Define 'keen' in context: She is keen to learn new skills.

instruction: Define 'novel' in context: The scientist proposed a novel approach to the problem.

instruction: Define 'obsolete' in context: Fax machines are becoming obsolete.

instruction: Provide synonyms and antonyms for 'happy'.

instruction: Provide synonyms and antonyms for 'big'.
response: Synonyms: large, enormous, vast. Antonyms: small, tiny, miniature. Usage notes: 'Enormous' and 'vast' suggest extreme size; 'large' is more neutral.

instruction: Provide synonyms and antonyms for 'fast'.
response: Synonyms: quick, rapid, swift. Antonyms: slow, sluggish, leisurely. Usage notes: 'Swift' often implies elegance; 'rapid' suggests urgency; 'quick' is most general.

instruction: Provide synonyms and antonyms for 'smart'.
response: Synonyms: intelligent, clever, astute. Antonyms: foolish, ignorant, obtuse. Usage notes: 'Astute' implies shrewd judgment; 'clever' suggests resourcefulness; 'intelligent' is most neutral.

instruction: Provide synonyms and antonyms for 'brave'.
response: Synonyms: courageous, valiant, bold. Antonyms: cowardly, timid, fearful. Usage notes: 'Valiant' has a heroic connotation; 'bold' can also mean daring in a non-dangerous sense.

instruction: Provide synonyms and antonyms for 'old'.
response: Synonyms: ancient, aged, elderly. Antonyms: young, new, modern. Usage notes: 'Ancient' refers to very old things; 'elderly' is respectful for people; 'aged' is more neutral.

instruction: Provide synonyms and antonyms for 'difficult'.
response: Synonyms: challenging, arduous, complex. Antonyms: easy, simple, straightforward. Usage notes: 'Arduous' implies physical effort; 'complex' implies many parts; 'challenging' is most neutral.

instruction: What does the bracketed word mean in this sentence? The dog's [bark] echoed through the empty street.
response: 'Bark' here is the sound a dog makes. It does not refer to the outer covering of a tree.

instruction: What does the bracketed word mean in this sentence? She gave a friendly [wave] as she passed.
response: 'Wave' here is a gesture of greeting. It does not refer to a wave of water or a wave of emotion.

instruction: What does the bracketed word mean in this sentence? The sales team prepared their [pitch] for the investors.
response: 'Pitch' here means a persuasive presentation. It does not refer to a sports field or the pitch of a sound.

instruction: What does the bracketed word mean in this sentence? She remained [cool] under pressure during the interview.
response: 'Cool' here means calm and composed. It does not refer to temperature or to something fashionable.

instruction: What does the bracketed word mean in this sentence? The match ended in a [draw].
response: 'Draw' here means a tied result. It does not mean to sketch or to pull something.

instruction: Give me 10 key vocabulary words related to 'medicine' with definitions and examples.
response:
diagnosis: the identification of a disease. Example: The doctor made a diagnosis after reviewing the test results.
prognosis: the likely course of a disease. Example: The prognosis for recovery was positive.
chronic: persisting for a long time. Example: She suffers from a chronic back condition.
acute: severe and sudden in onset. Example: He was admitted for acute appendicitis.
benign: not harmful; not cancerous. Example: The tumour was found to be benign.
malignant: tending to invade and destroy tissue. Example: The malignant cells spread rapidly.
remission: a temporary recovery from disease. Example: The patient went into remission after treatment.
symptom: a sign of a condition experienced by the patient. Example: Fever is a common symptom of infection.
pathogen: a bacterium or virus causing disease. Example: The pathogen was identified in the lab.
immunisation: the process of making immune to infection. Example: Childhood immunisation prevents many diseases.

instruction: Give me 10 key vocabulary words related to 'technology' with definitions and examples.
response:
algorithm: a set of rules for solving a problem. Example: The search engine uses a complex algorithm.
bandwidth: the capacity of a network connection. Example: Streaming video requires high bandwidth.
encryption: converting data into a coded form. Example: All messages are protected by encryption.
latency: the delay before data transfer begins. Example: Low latency is critical for online gaming.
scalability: the ability to handle growing amounts of work. Example: The system was designed for scalability.
interface: a point where two systems meet and interact. Example: The user interface was redesigned for simplicity.
protocol: a set of rules governing data exchange. Example: HTTP is the protocol used for web communication.
cache: stored data for quick access. Example: Clearing the cache can resolve many browser issues.
iteration: repetition of a process to generate results. Example: Each iteration of the software improved performance.
deployment: the process of making software available. Example: The deployment was completed without downtime.

instruction: Give me 10 key vocabulary words related to 'law' with definitions and examples.
response:
jurisdiction: the official power to make legal decisions. Example: The case fell outside the court's jurisdiction.
litigation: the process of taking legal action. Example: The company faced costly litigation.
precedent: an earlier event used as a guide. Example: The ruling set a precedent for future cases.
plaintiff: the party who brings a case to court. Example: The plaintiff sought damages for negligence.
defendant: the party accused in a legal case. Example: The defendant pleaded not guilty.
statute: a written law passed by a legislature. Example: The statute was amended to reflect modern standards.
liability: the state of being legally responsible. Example: The company admitted liability for the accident.
injunction: a court order requiring action or restraint. Example: An injunction was granted to stop the publication.
affidavit: a written statement confirmed by oath. Example: She submitted an affidavit supporting her claim.
arbitration: settling a dispute outside court. Example: The parties agreed to resolve the matter through arbitration.

diagnosis: the identification of a disease. Example: The doctor made a diagnosis after reviewing the test results.
prognosis: the likely course of a disease. Example: The prognosis for recovery was positive.
chronic: persisting for a long time. Example: She suffers from a chronic back condition.
acute: severe and sudden in onset. Example: He was admitted for acute appendicitis.
benign: not harmful; not cancerous. Example: The tumour was found to be benign.
malignant: tending to invade and destroy tissue. Example: The malignant cells spread rapidly.
remission: a temporary recovery from disease. Example: The patient went into remission after treatment.
symptom: a sign of a condition experienced by the patient. Example: Fever is a common symptom of infection.
pathogen: a bacterium or virus causing disease. Example: The pathogen was identified in the lab.
immunisation: the process of making immune to infection. Example: Childhood immunisation prevents many diseases.

instruction: Give me 10 key vocabulary words related to 'technology' with definitions and examples.
response:
algorithm: a set of rules for solving a problem. Example: The search engine uses a complex algorithm.
bandwidth: the capacity of a network connection. Example: Streaming video requires high bandwidth.
encryption: converting data into a coded form. Example: All messages are protected by encryption.
latency: the delay before data transfer begins. Example: Low latency is critical for online gaming.
scalability: the ability to handle growing amounts of work. Example: The system was designed for scalability.
interface: a point where two systems meet and interact. Example: The user interface was redesigned for simplicity.
protocol: a set of rules governing data exchange. Example: HTTP is the protocol used for web communication.
cache: stored data for quick access. Example: Clearing the cache can resolve many browser issues.
iteration: repetition of a process to generate results. Example: Each iteration of the software improved performance.
deployment: the process of making software available. Example: The deployment was completed without downtime.

instruction: Give me 10 key vocabulary words related to 'law' with definitions and examples.
response:
jurisdiction: the official power to make legal decisions. Example: The case fell outside the court's jurisdiction.
litigation: the process of taking legal action. Example: The company faced costly litigation.
precedent: an earlier event used as a guide. Example: The ruling set a precedent for future cases.
plaintiff: the party who brings a case to court. Example: The plaintiff sought damages for negligence.
defendant: the party accused in a legal case. Example: The defendant pleaded not guilty.
statute: a written law passed by a legislature. Example: The statute was amended to reflect modern standards.
liability: the state of being legally responsible. Example: The company admitted liability for the accident.
injunction: a court order requiring action or restraint. Example: An injunction was granted to stop the publication.
affidavit: a written statement confirmed by oath. Example: She submitted an affidavit supporting her claim.
arbitration: settling a dispute outside court. Example: The parties agreed to resolve the matter through arbitration.

liability: the state of being legally responsible. Example: The company admitted liability for the accident.
injunction: a court order requiring action or restraint. Example: An injunction was granted to stop the publication.
affidavit: a written statement confirmed by oath. Example: She submitted an affidavit supporting her claim.
arbitration: settling a dispute outside court. Example: The parties agreed to resolve the matter through arbitration.

instruction: Give me 10 key vocabulary words related to 'medicine' with definitions and examples.
response:
diagnosis: the identification of a disease. Example: The doctor made a diagnosis after reviewing the test results.
prognosis: the likely course of a disease. Example: The prognosis for recovery was positive.
chronic: persisting for a long time. Example: She suffers from a chronic back condition.
acute: severe and sudden in onset. Example: He was admitted for acute appendicitis.
benign: not harmful; not cancerous. Example: The tumour was found to be benign.
malignant: tending to invade and destroy tissue. Example: The malignant cells spread rapidly.
remission: a temporary recovery from disease. Example: The patient went into remission after treatment.
symptom: a sign of a condition experienced by the patient. Example: Fever is a common symptom of infection.
pathogen: a bacterium or virus causing disease. Example: The pathogen was identified in the lab.
immunisation: the process of making immune to infection. Example: Childhood immunisation prevents many diseases.

instruction: Give me 10 key vocabulary words related to 'technology' with definitions and examples.
response:
algorithm: a set of rules for solving a problem. Example: The search engine uses a complex algorithm.
bandwidth: the capacity of a network connection. Example: Streaming video requires high bandwidth.
encryption: converting data into a coded form. Example: All messages are protected by encryption.
latency: the delay before data transfer begins. Example: Low latency is critical for online gaming.
scalability: the ability to handle growing amounts of work. Example: The system was designed for scalability.
interface: a point where two systems meet and interact. Example: The user interface was redesigned for simplicity.
protocol: a set of rules governing data exchange. Example: HTTP is the protocol used for web communication.
cache: stored data for quick access. Example: Clearing the cache can resolve many browser issues.
iteration: repetition of a process to generate results. Example: Each iteration of the software improved performance.
deployment: the process of making software available. Example: The deployment was completed without downtime.

'break a record': She broke the world record in the 100m sprint.
'break the news': He had to break the news of the accident to the family.
'break a habit': It is hard to break a bad habit.
'break the ice': A joke can help break the ice at a meeting.
'break a promise': He never breaks a promise.

instruction: Give 5 natural collocations for the word 'make' with example sentences.
response:
'make a decision': She needs to make a decision by Friday.
'make progress': The team is making good progress on the project.
'make an effort': He made a real effort to improve his grades.
'make a mistake': Everyone makes mistakes; what matters is learning from them.
'make a difference': Volunteering can make a real difference in people's lives.

instruction: Give 5 natural collocations for the word 'take' with example sentences.
response:
'take a break': Let's take a break and come back to this later.
'take responsibility': She took responsibility for the error.
'take action': The government must take action on climate change.
'take a risk': Starting a business means taking a risk.
'take advantage': He took advantage of the opportunity to travel.

instruction: Give 5 natural collocations for the word 'strong' with example sentences.
response:
'strong evidence': There is strong evidence to support the theory.
'strong opinion': She has a strong opinion on the matter.
'strong coffee': He prefers strong coffee in the morning.
'strong wind': A strong wind knocked over the fence.
'strong argument': The lawyer presented a strong argument.

instruction: Give 5 natural collocations for the word 'heavy' with example sentences.
response:
'heavy rain': Heavy rain caused flooding in the city.
'heavy traffic': We were delayed by heavy traffic on the motorway.
'heavy workload': She struggled with a heavy workload this month.
'heavy smoker': He was a heavy smoker for twenty years.
'heavy emphasis': The course places heavy emphasis on practical skills.
